title: The missing 500 million: Cosmic bombardment melted Earth's first crust
gist: Curtin University geologist Tim Johnson argues Earth's continents exist because sustained asteroid bombardment in the Hadean kept the crust thin and molten; impact heating exceeded internal heat by roughly an order of magnitude until impacts faded 3.9-3.5 billion years ago, letting crust thicken and plate tectonics start.

insights.claim: A Curtin University team argues continent formation on Earth was caused largely by an intense, sustained barrage of asteroid impacts that kept the early crust hot and thin enough to make buoyant continents possible.
insights.speaker: Jacek Krywko
insights.so_what: A new causal model for how habitable land arose; watch for follow-on validation in Science that could reshape the consensus on early-Earth geodynamics and origin-of-life timing.
insights.quote: the lands we live on are here because of ancient bombardment from space
insights.locator: Johnson and his colleagues are now arguing

insights.claim: Earth is the only known planet with buoyant, silica-rich continents, and after decades of research geologists still do not agree on how or why they began forming around four billion years ago.
insights.speaker: Jacek Krywko
insights.so_what: Establishes the open scientific problem the impact model claims to solve; the lack of consensus is what makes a new mechanism high-impact if it holds.
insights.quote: The continents started appearing around about four billion years ago—that’s the oldest continental rock we know about
insights.locator: Earth is the only planet we know of

insights.claim: Most prior modeling treated internal sources—leftover accretion and core-formation heat plus radioactive decay—as absolutely dominant in the early Earth's heat budget, but the impact model shows they were not.
insights.speaker: Jacek Krywko
insights.so_what: The contrarian move is adding external energy that everyone omitted; it is the crux that makes the whole hypothesis work or fail.
insights.quote: Nobody could make it fit because we did not consider the energy coming from outside of Earth.
insights.locator: The issue with both these ideas

insights.claim: When impact contributions were summed, impact heating exceeded radiogenic and core heat for most of the Hadean by roughly an order of magnitude.
insights.speaker: Jacek Krywko
insights.so_what: A ~10x differential is the quantitative claim reviewers will stress-test; it is the number that separates this from earlier failed heat-budget models.
insights.quote: impact heating exceeded radiogenic and core heat for most of the Hadean by roughly an order of magnitude
insights.locator: When Johnson and his colleagues added up these contributions

insights.claim: Each impact's kinetic energy converts to heat: some vaporizes or melts rock at the site, but for big impactors most propagates into and heats the entire upper mantle, driving basaltic volcanism over tens to hundreds of millions of years after the collision.
insights.speaker: Jacek Krywko
insights.so_what: The long tail of post-impact heating is what makes sporadic collisions sustain a continuously hot crust—the mechanism doing the real work.
insights.quote: This energy basically heats up the entire upper mantle
insights.locator: The team focused on modeling how the kinetic energy

insights.claim: Because the Moon lacks plate tectonics its crust is a single continuous shell peppered with datable craters, making it the only reliable record of what was bombarding the Earth-Moon system four billion years ago.
insights.speaker: Jacek Krywko
insights.so_what: Shows the method leans on Apollo-era lunar samples as ground truth; new lunar sample-return missions could tighten or upend the calibration.
insights.quote: Because the Moon does not have plate tectonics, its crust is a single, solid, continuous shell.

insights.claim: Scaling lunar crater-count flux up to Earth's larger size and stronger gravity implies the planet was hit by thousands of impactors greater than 10 kilometers in diameter.
insights.speaker: Jacek Krywko
insights.so_what: Quantifies the bombardment intensity; the >10 km, thousands-of-impactors figure is the input that drives the entire energy calculation.
insights.quote: the planet must have been hit by thousands of impactors that were greater than 10 kilometers in diameter

insights.claim: Geodynamic simulations with the reworked heat budget imply Hadean crust was less than 5 kilometers thick with widespread partial melting starting just 2 to 3 kilometers below the surface.
insights.speaker: Jacek Krywko
insights.so_what: Concrete depth/thickness numbers make the model falsifiable against any future physical samples of Hadean crust.
insights.quote: it was less than 5 kilometers thick, with widespread partial melting starting just 2 to 3 kilometers below the surface
insights.locator: Feeding this reworked heat budget into geodynamic simulations

insights.claim: At around 5 kilometers depth melt fractions exceeded 30 percent by volume, past the point where rock can hold together as a coherent slab, so rigid plates and subduction were physically impossible in the Hadean.
insights.speaker: Jacek Krywko
insights.so_what: This is the direct rebuttal to the competing 'plate tectonics ran in the Hadean' camp; the 30% melt threshold is the physical argument.
insights.quote: Subduction and plate tectonics require that your lithosphere is rigid and it can jostle around and subduct
insights.locator: The key takeaway was that plate tectonics

insights.claim: Large impacts produced wholesale recycling of crust back into the mantle to depths of at least 600 kilometers, which Johnson argues explains why so little Hadean crust survived and why shock-deformed Hadean zircons are nearly absent.
insights.speaker: Jacek Krywko
insights.so_what: Offers a testable explanation for two puzzling gaps in the rock record; the shallow-melt shock-absorption claim is a distinctive prediction to check against zircon studies.
insights.quote: material dripping down to depths of at least 600 kilometers
insights.locator: The simulations that captured the localized effects

insights.claim: The impact flux declined roughly exponentially, and between 3.9 and 3.5 billion years ago it dropped enough that internal heat sources took over as the dominant control on the crust.
insights.speaker: Jacek Krywko
insights.so_what: Pins the crossover window that the model predicts should align with the first appearance of continental rocks—a key consistency check.
insights.quote: Between 3.9 and 3.5 billion years ago, it had dropped enough that internal heat sources took over
insights.locator: The impact flux didn’t stay high forever

insights.claim: As impact heating faded the upper mantle cooled and crust thickened to around 30 kilometers by the early Archean, finally rigid enough to support plate tectonics just as the first continental rocks appear in the record.
insights.speaker: Jacek Krywko
insights.so_what: The model's payoff: it ties crustal thickening, the onset of plate tectonics, and continent birth into one timeline that matches the ~4.03 Gya rock record.
insights.quote: As soon as you can create thick crust and you can create a mantle lithosphere underneath, you can start building continents
insights.locator: The team’s modeling suggests crustal thickness

insights.claim: The team concedes the argument rests largely on physics-based modeling rather than rock samples, with Johnson arguing that in the absence of surviving Hadean rocks the model outputs should be taken seriously rather than abandoned.
insights.speaker: Jacek Krywko
insights.so_what: Flags the central vulnerability: this is a modeling result with almost no physical corroboration, so conviction should be tempered pending rock evidence.
insights.quote: We need to start taking seriously the outputs of these models rather than just say, well, we can’t find any rocks, so let’s give up
insights.locator: The team admits much of the argument rests

insights.claim: The oldest known continental-type rocks crystallized around 4.03 billion years ago, rare basaltic rocks date to about 4.2 billion years, and the oldest zircon crystals push the record to 4.4 billion years, leaving almost nothing before that.
insights.speaker: Jacek Krywko
insights.so_what: The scarcity of pre-4 Gya rock is exactly why modeling substitutes for data here and why any new ancient-rock discovery is disproportionately valuable.
insights.quote: a handful of the oldest zircon crystals push the record back to 4.4 billion years

insights.claim: North American researchers recently dated a dark mafic rock in Canada's Nuvvuagittuq Greenstone Belt at 4.2 billion years old, and Johnson says another group has found a possibly even older rock to be reported within months.
insights.speaker: Jacek Krywko
insights.so_what: New Hadean-age rock finds are the near-term catalyst that could corroborate or challenge the impact-heating model; watch for the forthcoming older-rock paper.
insights.quote: a team of North American researchers has recently dated a dark, mafic rock as 4.2 billion years old
insights.locator: In Nuvvuagittuq Greenstone Belt in Canada
